attribution.name: PlayfulProcess
attribution.note: AI-assisted first-pass catalogue entry; for maintainer + Tarot History Forum review.

name: Mamluk Playing Cards — Mulūk wa-nuwwāb (the Direct Ancestor)
description:
The single most important upstream object in this repo: the four-suit pack Europe copied. The surviving deck — *Mulūk wa-nuwwāb* ("Kings and Deputies"), the game called *kanjifah* — is a Mamluk-era set (Egypt & Syria, dated to roughly the 15th century, c. 1500), of which **43 of an original 52 cards survive**, held at the **Topkapı Palace Museum, Istanbul** (found and published by L. A. Mayer, 1939/1971). It has **four suits** — cups, coins, swords, and **polo-sticks** — each running **Ace–10 plus three court cards** (a king and two deputies). The courts are **aniconic**: no human figures, only abstract design and calligraphic inscriptions naming the rank. When this pack reached Iberia and Italy in the **1370s** (as *naipes* / *naibi*), Italians later added a fifth suit of 21 triumphs + the Fool — and that addition, in the **1440s**, is tarot. So tarot is *this deck plus trumps*. The polo-sticks, a game Europeans didn't play, were redrawn as **batons/wands**; cups, coins and swords carried over directly.

GAME OR DIVINATION? A gaming/gambling object. The deepest roots of tarot are a card-game tradition (Tang-China money-suited cards → Mamluk *kanjifah* → European *naibi*); divination cultures existed alongside but used *other* instruments. See the meta's essay 'The Divination Question.'

ANCESTRAL STATUS: **direct ancestor** (the parent of the European four-suit pack), as distinct from the *cousin* branches (e.g. Ganjifa) that did not become tarot.

SOURCES: L. A. Mayer, *Mamluk Playing Cards* (1971); Michael Dummett, *The Game of Tarot* (1980); Topkapı Palace Museum, Istanbul; see also this repo's research/00c-islamic-and-chinese-card-origins.mdx.

---
*AI-assisted first-pass draft, pending review by the maintainer and the Tarot History Forum. Corrections welcome via pending edit.*

sections.What it is: A 52-card, four-suit pack (43 surviving) from the Mamluk Sultanate, in the Topkapı Palace Museum. The structure — four suits of ten pips plus three courts — is the skeleton Europe inherited.
sections.Why it matters: Tarot = this pack + a fifth suit of trumps. Remove the 21 triumphs and the Fool from any historical tarot and you are left with a Mamluk-shaped deck.
sections.Provenance: Found and published by L. A. Mayer (1939; monograph 1971). Held at the Topkapı Palace Museum, Istanbul.

sections.What it is: Ten pip cards (Ace–10) plus three courts. Carried into Europe directly as Cups (coppe / coupes).
Ten pips plus three courts. Carried into Europe as Coins (denari / deniers).
Ten pips plus three courts. The Mamluk swords are straight; carried into Europe as Swords (spade / épées).

Ten pips plus three courts. Polo was a Mamluk court sport Europeans did not play — so this suit was redrawn as Batons / Wands (bastoni / bâtons). The clearest single fingerprint of the Islamic origin.
Each suit has three court cards: the **malik** (king), the **nā'ib** (viceroy / deputy), and the **thānī nā'ib** (second deputy). Hence the game's name, *Mulūk wa-nuwwāb*, 'Kings and Deputies.'
sections.Aniconic: Following Islamic norms against figural images, the courts show no human figures — only abstract ornament and calligraphic inscriptions (rank names, sometimes verses of courtly praise). Europe later replaced the second deputy with a mounted Knight and added a Queen.

sections.What it is: Cups → Cups, Coins → Coins, Swords → Swords (Latin suits), and Polo-sticks → Batons. The Latin-suited packs (Italian/Spanish) preserve the Mamluk shapes most faithfully; the later French suits (hearts/tiles/clovers/pikes) are a simplification of these.

Playing cards most likely begin in Tang-dynasty China (money-suited cards). Through an undocumented transmission they reach the Islamic world as *kanjifah*; the Mamluk pack is the form that crossed into Iberia and Italy in the 1370s. Italy's fifth suit of triumphs (1440s) makes it tarot. The deepest roots are gaming, not divination.
